title: Driving wheel

infobox.value: is a powered wheel which is driven by the locomotive's [pistons](/wiki/Piston "Piston") (or [turbine](/wiki/Turbine "Turbine"), in the case of a [steam turbine locomotive](/wiki/Steam_turbine_locomotive "Steam turbine locomotive")).[[1]](#cite_note-1) On a conventional, non-articulated locomotive, the driving wheels are all coupled together with side rods (also known as [coupling rods](/wiki/Coupling_rod "Coupling rod")); normally one pair is directly driven by the [main rod](/wiki/Connecting_rod "Connecting rod"), or connecting rod, which is connected to the end of the [piston rod](/wiki/Piston_rod "Piston rod"); power is transmitted to the others through the side rods.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Powered wheel of a locomotive

This article is about the powered wheels on locomotives. For powered wheels on automobiles, see [drive wheel](/wiki/Drive_wheel "Drive wheel"). For steering control on automobiles known as that in the UK, see [Steering wheel](/wiki/Steering_wheel "Steering wheel").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/462driving.jpg/330px-462driving.jpg)](/wiki/File:462driving.jpg)

The driving wheels (boxed) on [Pennsylvania Railroad 1737](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad_1737 "Pennsylvania Railroad 1737")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Traction_motor_german-class140.jpg/330px-Traction_motor_german-class140.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traction_motor_german-class140.jpg)

Traction motor for a German locomotive

On a [steam locomotive](/wiki/Steam_locomotive "Steam locomotive"), a **driving wheel** is a powered wheel which is driven by the locomotive's [pistons](/wiki/Piston "Piston") (or [turbine](/wiki/Turbine "Turbine"), in the case of a [steam turbine locomotive](/wiki/Steam_turbine_locomotive "Steam turbine locomotive")).[[1]](#cite_note-1) On a conventional, non-articulated locomotive, the driving wheels are all coupled together with side rods (also known as [coupling rods](/wiki/Coupling_rod "Coupling rod")); normally one pair is directly driven by the [main rod](/wiki/Connecting_rod "Connecting rod"), or connecting rod, which is connected to the end of the [piston rod](/wiki/Piston_rod "Piston rod"); power is transmitted to the others through the side rods.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

On [diesel](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive") and [electric locomotives](/wiki/Electric_locomotive "Electric locomotive"), the driving wheels may be directly driven by the [traction motors](/wiki/Traction_motor "Traction motor"). Coupling rods are not usually used, and it is quite common for each axle to have its own motor. [Jackshaft](/wiki/Jackshaft_(locomotive) "Jackshaft (locomotive)") drive and coupling rods were used in the past[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) (e.g. in the [Swiss Crocodile locomotive](/wiki/Crocodile_(locomotive) "Crocodile (locomotive)")[[7]](#cite_note-7)) but their use is now confined to [shunter locomotives](/wiki/Switcher_locomotive "Switcher locomotive").

On an [articulated locomotive](/wiki/Articulated_locomotive "Articulated locomotive") or a [duplex locomotive](/wiki/Duplex_locomotive "Duplex locomotive"), driving wheels are grouped into sets with wheels within each set linked together.

## Diameter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Driving_wheel&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Diameter")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/MILW_261_drivers.jpg/330px-MILW_261_drivers.jpg)](/wiki/File:MILW_261_drivers.jpg)

Half of the driving wheels of [Milwaukee Road 261](/wiki/Milwaukee_Road_261 "Milwaukee Road 261")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/60163_Tornado_wheel.JPG/250px-60163_Tornado_wheel.JPG)](/wiki/File:60163_Tornado_wheel.JPG)

One of six 6 ft 8 in (2.03 m) driving wheels belonging to [60163 *Tornado*](/wiki/60163_Tornado "60163 Tornado")

Driving wheels are generally larger than [leading](/wiki/Leading_wheel "Leading wheel") or [trailing wheels](/wiki/Trailing_wheel "Trailing wheel"). Since a conventional steam locomotive is directly driven, one of the few ways to '[gear](/wiki/Gear_ratio "Gear ratio")' a locomotive for a particular performance goal is to size the driving wheels appropriately.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Freight locomotives generally had driving wheels between 40 and 60 inches (1,016 and 1,524 mm) in diameter; dual-purpose locomotives generally between 60 and 70 inches (1,524 and 1,778 mm), and passenger locomotives between 70 and 100 inches (1,778 and 2,540 mm) or so.

The driving wheels on express passenger locomotives have come down in diameter over the years, e.g. from 8 ft 1 in (2,464 mm) on the [GNR Stirling 4-2-2](/wiki/GNR_Stirling_4-2-2 "GNR Stirling 4-2-2") of 1870 to 6 ft 2 in (1,880 mm) on the [SR Merchant Navy Class](/wiki/SR_Merchant_Navy_Class "SR Merchant Navy Class") of 1941. This is because improvements in valve design allowed for higher piston speeds.

## Flangeless wheels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Driving_wheel&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Flangeless wheels")]

Some long-wheelbase locomotives (four or more coupled axles) were equipped with **blind drivers**. These were driving wheels without the usual [flanges](/wiki/Flange "Flange"), which allowed them to negotiate tighter curves without binding.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Some three-driving-axle locomotives also had flangeless wheels on the middle axle, such as [Everett Railroad 11](/wiki/Everett_Railroad_11 "Everett Railroad 11"), and the [NZR WH class](/wiki/NZR_WH_class "NZR WH class").

## Balancing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Driving_wheel&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Balancing")]

Main article: [Engine balance](/wiki/Engine_balance "Engine balance")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Steam_locomotive_driving_wheel.jpg/330px-Steam_locomotive_driving_wheel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Steam_locomotive_driving_wheel.jpg)

A flangeless driving wheel on a [steam locomotive](/wiki/Steam_locomotive "Steam locomotive")

On locomotives with [side rods](/wiki/Side_rod "Side rod"), including most [steam](/wiki/Steam_locomotive "Steam locomotive") and [jackshaft](/wiki/Jackshaft_(locomotive) "Jackshaft (locomotive)") locomotives, the driving wheels have weights to balance the weight of the coupling and connecting rods.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The crescent-shaped balance weight is clearly visible in the illustrative image.

## Whyte notation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Driving_wheel&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Whyte notation")]

In the [Whyte notation](/wiki/Whyte_notation "Whyte notation"), driving wheels are designated by the middle number or numbers in the set.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERansome-Wallis2001505-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The [UIC classification](/wiki/UIC_classification "UIC classification") system counts the number of axles rather than the number of wheels and driving wheels are designated by letters rather than numbers. The suffix 'o' is used to indicate independently-powered axles.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The number of driving wheels on locomotives varied quite a bit. Some early locomotives had as few as two driving wheels (one axle). The largest number of total driving wheels was 24 (twelve axles) on the [2-8-8-8-2](/wiki/2-8-8-8-2 "2-8-8-8-2") and [2-8-8-8-4](/wiki/2-8-8-8-4 "2-8-8-8-4") locomotives. The largest number of coupled driving wheels was 14 (seven axles) on the ill-fated AA20 [4-14-4](/wiki/4-14-4 "4-14-4") locomotive.

## Other uses of the term *driving wheel*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Driving_wheel&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Other uses of the term driving wheel")]

The term *driving wheel* is sometimes used to denote the *[drive sprocket](/wiki/Sprocket "Sprocket")* which moves the [track](/wiki/Caterpillar_track "Caterpillar track") on tracked vehicles such as [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank") and [bulldozers](/wiki/Bulldozer "Bulldozer").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Driving_wheel&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Many American roots artists, including [The Byrds](/wiki/The_Byrds "The Byrds"), [Tom Rush](/wiki/Tom_Rush "Tom Rush"), [The Black Crowes](/wiki/The_Black_Crowes "The Black Crowes") and [Cowboy Junkies](/wiki/Cowboy_Junkies "Cowboy Junkies"), have performed a song written by [David Wiffen](/wiki/David_Wiffen "David Wiffen") called "Driving Wheel", with the lyrics "I feel like some old engine/ That's lost my driving wheel."[[17]](#cite_note-17) These lyrics are a reference to the traditional blues song "Broke Down Engine Blues" by [Blind Willie McTell](/wiki/Blind_Willie_McTell "Blind Willie McTell"), 1931.[[18]](#cite_note-18) It was later directly covered by [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") and [Johnny Winter](/wiki/Johnny_Winter "Johnny Winter").

Many versions of the American folk song "[In the Pines](/wiki/In_the_Pines "In the Pines")" performed by artists such as Leadbelly, [Mark Lanegan](/wiki/Mark_Lanegan "Mark Lanegan") (on *[The Winding Sheet](/wiki/The_Winding_Sheet "The Winding Sheet")*), and [Nirvana](/wiki/Nirvana_(band) "Nirvana (band)") (On *[MTV Unplugged In New York](/wiki/MTV_Unplugged_In_New_York "MTV Unplugged In New York")*) reference a decapitated man's head found in a driving wheel.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In addition, it is likely that Chuck Berry references the locomotive driving wheel in "Johnny B. Goode" when he sings, "the engineers would see him sitting in the shade / Strumming with the rhythm that the drivers made."
